data.title: Visa: Speculations Are Over, Buying Diversified IT And Finance In One (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
This February, I published the last review of Visa (NYSE:V) in the article Time To Sell Visa: Waiting For The Price To Drop For A New Long-Term Position. At that time, my rating of the company was sell, but despite the fact that the described scenario was fully implemented, the research left a dual impression on both the esteemed readers and me. The fact is that 3 months earlier, I published the first coverage of the company, which was quite bullish. And the last sell rating was formed due to a number of circumstances and was essentially speculative. I understand that the full picture was only in my head, and judging by the comments, it was not bought by the readers of Seeking Alpha.

2 quarterly reports since the last rating, having revised the system of assessing companies for a longer term, I upgrade Visa's rating to buy, based on a quantitative analysis of financial indicators and fundamental prerequisites for further business growth.

Thesis Disclaimer

I use a buy rating, but my approach would be better served by a rating scale from avoid to attractive. Attractive status or strong buy rating relative to V stock is based solely on an analysis of the financial performance, taking into account the current valuation as an additional factor. My opinion may be useful to investors considering whether to include the stock in a long-term portfolio and hold it for a 3-5 year horizon, and does not constitute a recommendation or trading instruction.

Valuation Context

When I published my last review, I based it on the current price of $351 per share, and warned of a possible correction of 14% to $301.8, which, for me, was a fair value based on a quantitative approach using the historical multiples method.

The scenario came true, and I returned to the ranks of the bulls. My quantitative approach involves fixing the trailing P/E TTM for the last 12 quarters on the day after the report is published. It should be noted that the market valuation of Visa is quite stable, and the spread of estimates is not large over this period: from 26x to 33x. This determines the proximity of both the median of the entire series of values ​​(which I take as the fair value) and the median of the upper and lower thirds of this sample, which serve as a benchmark for overvaluation and undervaluation for me.

Given the confident growth of EPS at the moment, all three levels have moved up, and the current fair value according to my method is $325.3, which is only 3% lower than the closing price as of Aug. 05. After touching the previous level for buying $301.8, the market gave a profit of more than 20%, but I will leave all speculations on the topic of speculation outside the review this time. The current 30x are in the market and leave an opportunity for medium-term and long-term investors for a long time.

Fundamentals

In Q3 FY25, international payments again showed double-digit growth of +16% YoY, especially due to tourist flows to Europe and Asia. This drives net revenue and outpaces the growth of the total payment volume.

Along with the steady growth of the cross-border segment, Visa is expanding B2B payment solutions (Visa Direct, Commercial Pay) and "value-added services" (fraud detection, data analytics, etc.) - together they already provide 60% of revenue and are growing faster than the main business segment of consumer payments. During Investor Day 2025, the company's management spoke about the importance of these areas and the opportunities that the company receives by developing them.

The last 2 quarterly reports only confirm the trend towards diversification and the focus on B2B & VAS in the context of C2B saturation.

The growth of the VAS and New Flows segments reduces the business's dependence on the cyclicality of consumption, transforming the company from a classic card network business into a multi-level payment platform with a high share of marginal areas. Every dollar of growth should be due to highly profitable services, not large-volume transactions.

The sustainable growth of cross-border transactions may be a temporary result of the recovery of international tourism after the pandemic, but it also provides greater stability for future financial results.

Quantitative Side

Against the backdrop of a changing revenue structure and shifting emphasis, it is interesting to see how financial indicators are reacting to this. Visa, based on the results of Q3'25, still has the most powerful financial performance metrics. In my first review, I think I formulated my understanding of the business very successfully:

The absence of R&D and low capital expenditures minimizes the risk of uncontrolled write-offs, making Visa an attractive blend of IT and financials with strong growth potential.

This allows the company to demonstrate record high or close to them values of net profit margin of 52% and ROIC of 40% (own calculations).

The company also has a high level of solvency, spending only 2% of operating profit on interest expenses (net interest expenses are positive). The cash flow from operating activities TTM of $23.b completely covers the total debt of $22b, calculated on the average for 4 quarters.

Ultimately, using my system for rating each company, I calculate an overall rating based on 8 financial metrics, and for Visa, it is 80% out of 100%.

Only one parameter (even with the greatest weight) deviated from the benchmark to the downside-this is the expected EPS growth against the historical one. If we rely on analysts' forecasts and the expected EPS of $14.46 by the end of September 2027, then the CAGR for the next 2 years will be ~12%, which is less than the actual one over the past 3 years (14.71%). Let the numbers be double-digit, and the deviation is not great, but I am strict in following the system, and for this, I deducted points from the overall rating.

Although I have a strong feeling that EPS will be higher. Firstly, the company has been regularly exceeding market expectations for 4 years in a row. Without exception.

Secondly, the last $30b buyback program has been authorized since April 2025. The program is almost half-fulfilled; stable FCF and low CapEx allow for full implementation, which supports EPS. Therefore, when I look at future growth rates, I write down "minus", keeping "plus" in mind.

Bottom Line

Visa remains one of the most reliable, attractive, and unique stocks in my portfolio. A mix of IT and finance, a high level of indicators at a valuation of ~30x makes it a high-quality long-term investment with a clear opportunity to manage the position based on the historical multiples method. Like any other, this idea has its risks. Antitrust prosecution and regulatory pressure are a permanent risk for a giant like Visa. For example, the adoption of the Credit Card Competition Act (CCCA) discussed in the US Congress in its current form may lead to a decrease in interchange fees and increased competition from banks and fintechs. Sustainable penetration of new payment technologies may collide with competition from real-time payment systems, especially in emerging markets. But weighing these risks and the arguments above, I choose a long-term buy.

data.title: Arbor Realty: I Own The 8% Yielding Preferreds Over The Commons
data.description:
Arbor Realty's (ABR) dividend stability and growth had for years rendered the mortgage REIT one of the best tickers in the space. However, a recent dividend cut and slipping coverage have kept me neutral on the common shares even as I continue to build my position in the preferred shares. The mREIT generated fiscal 2025 second-quarter revenue of $68.73 million, down 21.9% from its year-ago comp and a miss by $8.29 million on consensus estimates. The dip needs to be contextualized. ABR's structured loan portfolio had an unpaid principal balance ("UPB") of $11.61 billion as of the end of the second quarter, but was down 2.2% from $11.87 billion a year ago. Further, total delinquencies came in at $529 million as of the end of the second quarter. This is defined as loans greater than 60 days past due and loans where ABR is not recording interest income on but are less than 60 days past due.

The first tranche of these non-performing loans ("NPL") constituted 89.2% of second-quarter NPLs at $471.8 million spread across nineteen NPLs. This dipped from $676.2 million spread across 24 loans a year ago. ABR last declared a quarterly cash dividend of $0.30 per share, kept unchanged from its prior distribution and annualized at $1.20 per share for a 10.3% dividend yield. This is lower than the trailing-twelve-month yield of 13.62%. Critically, ABR generated distributable EPS of $0.25 during the second quarter, down 3 cents sequentially and a drop from EPS of $0.45 a year ago. This means the dividend is only being 83% covered during the second quarter, or a roughly 120% payout ratio. I last covered ABR in May with a neutral rating, flagging the dividend cut and uncertainty around coverage as reasons to stay muted on the commons.

Originations, Weighted Average Yield, And Dividend Coverage

ABR is not pulling back on the origination of new multifamily-backed loans even as some of its core geographical markets go through some weakness. The UPB of the mREIT's structured loan portfolio had originations of $716.5 million during the second quarter against runoff of $519.7 million. This meant net funding of $197 million. The weighted average yield on the UPB of the structured loan portfolio at 7.95% as of the end of the second quarter had also increased by 16 basis points from 7.79% a year ago. Hence, ABR continues to display strength in overall origination volume even as its NPLs remain elevated but down from its year-ago comp and as originations maintain positive net fundings.

The risk here is that slipping coverage portends another dividend cut. The mREIT's trailing-twelve-month free cash flow as of the end of the second quarter stood at $342.23 million, a deficit of around $27.77 million from the trailing-twelve-month total dividends paid of $370 million. ABR's management stated during their earnings call that they're set to start growing earnings and the dividend again from 2026 as they work through resolving NPLs. The year-over-year drop is encouraging, but the lack of common dividend coverage keeps me on the sidelines.

The Preferreds And Widening Credit Spread Risk

Rents have been falling across the U.S., with 23 metro areas experiencing falling rents in June. This dip has been more pronounced in states like Texas, which forms ABR's most salient geographic market. I have a large position in the 6.25% Series E Preferreds (NYSE:ABR.PR.E) to maintain exposure to ABR's sprawling multifamily-backed loan portfolio. I like the comfort of sitting higher up the capital stack to hedge the risk of possible reductions to my income.

The Series E, like the other two preferreds, at $17.50 per share is trading at a 30% discount to its $25 per share liquidation value. This discount slims to 28.5% for owners of the Series D (NYSE:ABR.PR.D), slimming further to 11.6% for the fixed-to-floating rate Series F preferreds (NYSE:ABR.PR.F). Crucially, the Series E pays out a $1.5625 per share annual coupon for a 9% yield on cost. While this is roughly 230 basis points less than the dividend yield on the common shares, it's 377 basis points more than the current U.S. 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y). Hence, they face the risk of a widening of credit spreads if the U.S. were to fall into a recession or broad economic weakness. However, the odds of a US recession are falling. JPMorgan (JPM) initially placed this at 60% in April, dropping to 40% in May. U.S. real GDP also expanded by 3% in the second quarter of 2025, beating expectations and likely eliminating the likelihood of a recession in 2025. I'm confident in the ability of the mREIT to work through NPLs in the long term, but the commons are not a clear buy at this stage. This is being rated as a hold.

data.title: Forget The S&P 500, Here's Where I'm Investing Instead
data.description:
The S&P500 (SPY) has been on a hot streak.

Since early 2020, it has more than doubled investors' money, earning a ≈15% average annual return:

REITs (VNQ), on the other hand, have done very poorly during the past five years. Including dividends, they have delivered only slightly positive total returns, but ignoring dividends, they have been in a quasi-bear market for the last five years:

As a result, lots of investors are today happily investing in the S&P 500 and ignoring REITs.

But this ignores that the last five years have been highly unusual. We had the pandemic, a historic surge in interest rates, and a huge tech tailwind from the AI revolution. The S&P 500 saw its P/E multiple expand significantly, whereas REITs saw their valuation multiples compress to historic lows.

Extending the time period to 50-plus years, we find that REITs have actually outperformed the S&P 500 and even tech stocks:

I think that going forward, REITs are likely to regain the lead because their valuations have dropped to the lowest level in a decade relative to the S&P 500, and they should enjoy significant capital flows as interest rates are cut down to lower levels:

With that in mind, let's look at a few REITs that could potentially earn far higher returns than the S&P 500 going forward. They have all massively outperformed the past, and we expect those superior returns to continue.

EastGroup Properties (EGP)

EastGroup Properties has been one of the best-performing industrial REITs over the long run, massively outperforming even the S&P 500:

It has done so well because it owns a Class A portfolio of last-mile, high-barrier-to-entry warehouses and distribution centers in rapidly growing markets.

These properties have greatly benefited from the growth of e-commerce, the growing trend of onshoring, and rapid population growth in the sunbelt over the years.

This has allowed it to grow its rents, all while developing new properties, earning large spreads over its cost of capital.

And we think that the REIT is well positioned to keep outperforming going forward.

Firstly, the fundamentals of its properties remain strong. Some sub-sectors of the industrial market are today overbuilt, but EGP specializes in smaller urban properties with high barriers to entry. Those have seen little new supply in recent years:

But while the supply is limited, we expect the demand for these properties to increase as the AI revolution takes hold. We expect it to accelerate the growth of e-commerce as it will make digital marketing so much more powerful, and we expect it to also boost the trend of onshoring, as AI and robotics reduce the cost of domestic supply chains.

EGP's properties are also ideally located in rapidly growing markets, with 60% of its revenue coming from Texas and Florida:

EGP's balance sheet is also historically strong with just a ~10% LTV, leaving ample capacity to take on new debt to develop additional properties as opportunities present themselves.

As a result, analysts expect the REIT to keep growing at a high single-digit growth rate for years to come, reaching $12.47 of FFO per share by 2029.

Yet, today, you can buy it at a near 10-year low valuation, trading at just 17x its current FFO and 12.8x its 2029 forward FFO. It's even cheaper than at the bottom of the COVID crash right now.

Just for context, it traded at 35x FFO in early 2022 before the start of this REIT bear market. Incredible what a difference a few years can make!

35x FFO might have been a bit much, but 17x is also too low for a REIT with so little leverage, such high-quality assets, and solid long-term growth prospects.

We estimate fair value at closer to 25x FFO, and we expect it to get there as interest rates return to lower levels and capital flows back to the REIT sector. This has the potential to unlock 30% upside, and while you wait, you earn a 3.5% dividend yield, which, coupled with an 8% annual growth rate, should be plenty to earn double-digit total returns.

That's exactly what the REIT has delivered for 30-plus years in a row, and everything indicates that this streak should continue.

Sun Communities (SUI)

Sun Communities is one of our favorite residential REITs at High Yield Landlord, and it has also significantly outperformed the broader market over the long run, including the S&P 500.

It specializes in manufactured housing communities and RV parks, providing affordable housing solutions in desirable locations to its residents.

We think that it is particularly well-positioned right now after recently selling its marina business to Blackstone (BX). This transaction has allowed it to significantly deleverage its balance sheet and positions it for faster growth going forward:

Even then, the REIT currently trades at a historically low valuation, trading at just 17x FFO, which is a particularly low multiple for a manufactured housing REIT.

For context, it traded at 33x FFO prior to this bear market.

Once again, 33x was likely a bit rich, but 17x is too low. Fair value is likely somewhere in the middle for such a high-quality REIT with low leverage and bright prospects. The management has been funding buybacks with a portion of the proceeds from the recent asset sales to take advantage of this opportunity. We see fair value at closer to 25x FFO, meaning that the stock has about 30% upside potential.

While you wait, you earn a 3.5% dividend yield, which is growing rapidly. The REIT just recently hiked its dividend by 11% and also announced a special dividend.

Between the yield, the growth, and the upside, investors can expect to earn 12-15% average annual returns for years to come, which is in line with what they have achieved over the past decades.

Risk

The main risk to the thesis is that interest rates surge to higher levels. In that scenario, these two REITs could underperform the S&P 500, at least in the near term, as REITs have been highly correlated to interest rates over the past few years.

The debt market is today predicting significant rate cuts within the next year, which only strengthens our thesis, but if you disagree and expect interest rates to rise, then you may want to limit your exposure to REITs.

Closing Note

The S&P 500 has been hot lately, but past recent returns are not an indication of future performance. On the contrary, the strong recent performance could come at the cost of future results as the P/E multiple cannot keep expanding indefinitely.

REITs, on the other hand, are setting the stage for a strong recovery following many years of underperformance. This recovery could happen already in the near term as interest rates are cut, and we are positioning ourselves at High Yield Landlord to profit from this opportunity.

data.title: ULTY: An ETF Most Long-Term Investors Should Probably Avoid
data.description:
In a market where several dividend stocks offer a 3% or 4% dividend yield per year, an ETF such as YieldMax Ultra Option Income Strategy ETF (NYSEARCA:ULTY), which has “Ultra Option Income” in its name and has achieved a dividend yield of 126% over the last 12 months, really stands out. And okay, being an “income seeker” may make sense for some portfolios, but there are relevant caveats to be made about this ETF.

Although it is managing to accomplish its objective, it remains a complex, high-risk vehicle, and I would say that it does not make sense for most long-term investors, since it is a tool for generating cash flow in the short term and not compounding capital, and this comes with a relevant downside risk due to exposure to riskier and more volatile assets.

ULTY Is A “Yield-Chasing” Machine

ULTY is quite different from more traditional vehicles such as JEPQ or JEPI. Instead of following a more traditional index and writing options against it, ULTY selects a portfolio of 15 to 30 stocks “primarily on the basis of such securities’ implied volatility levels.”

That is the key information. High volatility stocks generally mean high option premiums, so the portfolio naturally gravitates toward more controversial and speculative stocks, and to generate income through options and consequently sustain a high dividend yield, this strategy works.

A quick look at its top 10 holdings confirms this. Many names related to the high-risk, high-reward vision, such as crypto-related companies like Marathon Digital (MARA) and Coinbase (COIN), speculative tech companies like Upstart (UPST) and IonQ (IONQ), and high-growth names like Nebius (NBIS). Having this portfolio would generate immense volatility, but systematically selling covered call options against these positions ends up reducing volatility, and diversification helps reduce downside risk.

At the same time, I don't mean to say that all of these positions are bad. Companies like Coinbase are delivering good results and executing strategies such as revenue diversification and other types of monetization via services in an interesting way for shareholders, but it is still a company that is dependent on the crypto market and has a nebulous valuation, i.e., it has both upside risk and significant downside risk.

Nebius is another good example. Q2 was pretty strong, with revenue growth of 600% YoY, making it clearer that the company is really building an ecosystem for secular trends such as AI. However, in addition to being a volatile name with a more uncertain character, ULTY also loses the upside that the company can generate, since in the last few days alone, the stock has risen more than 25%.

Marathon is an example where quality is more debatable. I don't want to say whether or not it's worth having the stock now, since I don't know the company well enough to draw up a model, but the fact is that Marathon, even though it is a crypto miner, has shown much higher volatility than Bitcoin in recent times, in addition to having declined more than 80% since its IPO.

And even if it manages to reduce volatility, as the chart below shows, shareholders need to keep in mind that the nature of the underlying assets is quite uncertain.

ULTY’s Appeal For The Income Seeker

To summarize the above section, ULTY can be good for a very specific type of investor. In my view, it is for those who, in addition to wanting to generate cash flow through capital, also understand and accept the risks, which are being exposed to companies with a cloudy future but which, in addition to enhancing income, also manage to gain exposure to exciting (and riskier) sectors, such as AI, quantum, and crypto.

Therefore, I believe that in an income-oriented portfolio, having a small and tactical allocation to ULTY can be interesting at times. That said, a caveat is very necessary here. This is by no means a buy-and-forget investment; it is the type that requires constant monitoring due to the high-risk nature of the underlying securities and because the composition and risk profile can change.

The Problem with ULTY for Long-Term Investors

The main problem is that for investors whose goal is to achieve high total returns over the long term, ULTY's strategy is not the most appropriate.

The NAV chart clearly shows this. Initially, the net asset value was almost $20, and now it is $6. The main source of income is options, so if the capital appreciation of the stocks plus this income is not enough to cover the massive distribution of capital (in addition to the 1.3% expense ratio), the NAV will naturally continue to fall over time. It is as if, in some periods, you are being paid with your own money.

The good part is that even with this decline, the total return was positive, but still below indexes such as the QQQ, meaning that the trade-off between cash flow via dividends and total return in this period was clear.

The Bottom Line

It is especially difficult to rate ETFs such as ULTY. It is a vehicle that can be used for some types of investors if done with caution, but its purpose is also very specific.

For most investors, who are focused on compounding capital over time, ULTY, in my view, is just a distraction that trades total return for income, and still has a significant downside risk.

data.title: Funds Only Portfolio: Potentially $6,000 Per Month Income
data.description:
Introduction:

There are three types of dividend investors:

  • Those who invest solely in funds.
  • Those who like to invest in individual stocks mostly for many reasons (discussed later).
  • Then there is the third category of investors who like to mix both individual stocks and funds.

Obviously, there are pros and cons to each approach. Let's talk about funds -only investors. These are folks who prefer a somewhat hands-off approach. They do not like to spend too much time or worry about any one particular stock. Some of them do not have time to research individual stocks, while others do not have the inclination. They would rather spend their time on their hobbies that they love to pursue. Another advantage of a funds-only portfolio is the wide diversification that one gets, even with a small number of funds. Sure, over-diversification leads to market-like performance, but not everyone is looking for 10-baggers, especially during the retirement years. For all this convenience, the investor pays some level of fees, which can vary widely depending upon the type of funds you own. They can typically vary between 0.06% for broad index funds to 0.50% for actively managed ETFs (Exchange Traded Funds). The fees can be even higher for specialized funds or CEFs (closed-end funds). The leveraged funds also add the interest expenses. However, the investor does not pay these fees directly, but they are taken out of the investor's returns. Over time, these fees can add up significantly.

The second type of investor likes to dabble in individual stocks mostly for two reasons. First, they do not like to pay fund fees on an ongoing basis. But more importantly, they do not mind spending time researching stocks. Also, they are likely more tolerant of the higher risk that comes with individual stocks. Also, zero commissions for trading stocks have made it easier for anyone to own and diversify in a relatively large number of individual stocks.

The third category of investors is those who like both of the approaches and usually have two buckets: a bucket of funds and a second one of individual stocks. Personally, we like this approach, but everyone should decide for themselves what suits them best.

That being said, this article is about a portfolio of funds, even though we regularly write about other strategies as well.

Why Is It Easier To Create A SWAN Portfolio With Funds:

In the financial world, a SWAN investment or portfolio is one that lets its owner sleep well at night. In other words, such a portfolio would mostly be an 'invest once and forget it' type. Ideally, such investments should keep compounding on a long-term basis, without worrying about them on a day-to-day or even monthly basis.

Sure, you can possibly have a SWAN portfolio with individual stocks, but it is likely much easier to do this with a funds-only portfolio. Since most funds have dozens (if not hundreds) of individual stocks or securities, they are highly diversified, resulting in lower volatility and drawdowns.

Why is it important to diversify in asset classes?

Most people know how important it is to diversify. No one stock can be good enough for all time to come, and no one gets it right all the time. That's why most Folks diversify. However, how do we plan for the right diversification? Warren Buffett has famously said about over-diversification: "Diversification is protection against ignorance. It makes little sense if you know what you are doing."

However, there are two important points here. First, his comment is about overdiversification. Secondly, most investors are not Warren Buffett. So, for most folks, a reasonable level of diversification is critically important. But we also want to add that it is also important to diversify not only among stocks but also into many different asset classes. Oftentimes, asset classes such as stocks, gold, real estate, and treasury bonds tend to move in different directions. So, if a portfolio has all four in some proportions, the overall volatility will come down substantially but still deliver solid performance over the long term (long term being the key).

Funds Only Portfolio:

We will focus this article mostly on funds that are selected from many asset classes.

Investment Plan:

The approach we are taking in this article is that this portfolio will be based on many asset classes. Once we have settled on the allocation of each asset type or the investment class, we will then select one or two funds under each class.

For the purpose of this article, we will assume that you are someone who likes to have the majority of your savings invested in funds. Also, you do not mind some (reasonable level) ongoing costs in lieu of active management and diversification that come with these funds.

Goals:

Goals should be based on your specific individual situation. Since we usually focus on retirees or folks who are a decade or so away from retirement, we will set the goals with these factors in mind.

With that, the first goal would be that such a portfolio should be able to provide a reasonably high income. What is reasonably high income? In our book, it is between 4% and 6%, depending on your need. Your portfolio may aim for even higher income, but the rest should be reinvested. The second goal is decent to good growth in terms of total returns. Everyone needs growth, even retirees in their late 70s or early 80s. Inflation is something that is a fact of life, and to beat inflation, our portfolio should be able to provide enough growth to cover the income and still beat inflation.

Here is the summary of our goals in order of priority:

• Provide 6% to 7% income (on average) on a consistent basis through good times and bad.

• Provide decent growth to be able to beat inflation. In other words, total returns should closely match the broader market over the long term.

• To conserve capital on a relative basis.

Portfolio Structure:

As stated earlier, most funds charge a management fee that directly cuts into the returns a fund provides. So, we should try to select funds that charge low fees. However, that is not always possible as we have to pay more for active management. Sometimes, a fund charging higher fees but having a very competent and effective management can deliver better returns compared to a low-fee passive fund. So, they should be analyzed on a host of factors, not just fees.

Funds can generally be of three types: ETFs (Exchange Traded Funds), CEFs (Closed-End Funds), and traditional mutual funds. Most ETFs do not carry any leverage, but many of the CEFs use leverage. For a moderately conservative portfolio, we should try to keep the leverage minimal.

To keep the portfolio diversified in terms of asset classes, we will aim for funds from many different sectors and asset classes. This will help keep the volatility low.

Below is a list of types (or sector and asset categories), from which we will select our funds:

  1. Dividend Growth (DGI) Funds
  2. Covered-call (Option) Strategies
  3. Real Estate funds
  4. Energy and Midstream MLPs
  5. Bond and Treasury funds
  6. Utilities & Infrastructure funds
  7. Municipal and Preferred Income
  8. Commodities & Materials
  9. Any Miscellaneous.

Table-1: Fund's Portfolio:

Note: There are two individual gold royalty company stocks, even though they themselves are invested in a large number of gold-related properties/assets.

Sector Allocation:

Chart-1: Sector Allocation

Backtesting Results:

Backtests can never predict the future, but can provide you with some idea of how a portfolio may behave under different market conditions. Moreover, we had to replace many funds with suitable alternates due to their lack of history prior to 2012/2013.

Chart-1A: Growth of $100,000 from year 2008-Jun.2025 (Backtesting)

Note: Many funds did not have histories prior to 2014 or 2012. These were suitably replaced for the prior years. However, JEPI and JEPQ had a very short history (4 years or less), and they were replaced by SPY and QQQX for the purpose of backtesting.

Discussion on the Selected Funds:

• DGI (Dividend Stocks) Funds

1) Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD)

SCHD is one of the most popular dividend ETFs among income investors. Its dividend yield is also reasonably high at 3.90% along with a very decent dividend growth record over the years. These two reasons together make it popular. The fund holds nearly 100 large-cap and blue-chip companies on a cap-weighted basis.

Some of the top holdings of SCHD are ConocoPhillips (COP), Chevron (CVX), Cisco Systems (CSCO), PepsiCo (PEP), Altria (MO), Amgen (AMGN), AbbVie (ABBV), Merck Co (MRK), Verizon (VZ), and Home Depot (HD).

2) Adams Diversified Equity Fund (ADX)

ADX is a closed-end fund with a very long history (incepted in Oct. 1929). Many of its holdings would be similar to the SCHD ETF. Its core philosophy is to invest in a broadly diversified, sector-neutral, large-cap equity portfolio. The fund is very well managed, and that is proven over the years.

The fund uses zero leverage and charges much lower fees than its peers. In the past 3, 5, and 10-year periods, it has returned on the NAV basis 16.75%, 15.12%, and 12.93%, respectively, on an annualized basis. Currently, the fund offers an -8% discount and yields roughly 8.6%.

• Bond Funds

We will select two funds under this category with a total allocation of 12%, divided equally between the two funds.

1) Fidelity Capital & Income Fund (FAGIX)

Now, most bond funds provide decent income with much lower volatility than stocks, but also come with much lower long-term returns. However, Fidelity's Capital & Income fund FAGIX is a bit different. Besides owning fixed income securities to a large extent, it has a history of some allocation to equities as well (roughly 15-10%). That strategy has provided the fund with very decent growth over the years. The fund has a long history, and as of Jan. 31, 2025, it has provided an annualized growth of 9.40% since its inception in 1977, which is solid for a bond-centric fund. This growth aspect makes this fund stand out, and that's why we often select it under the bonds category.

2) 20+ Year Treasury Fund ETF (TLT)

Currently, the interest rates are relatively high, and Treasury funds can yield 4.4% with relative safety. In fact, short-term funds are considered as good as cash, but long-term funds can gain or lose value depending on where the yield goes. If interest rates go down, yields would come down, but the price should go up. You could also consider iShares 7-10 Year Treasury ETF (IEF) or Vanguard Intermediate-term Treasury Fund (VGIT), as they consist of medium-term treasuries with a slightly lower yield of 3.75%. VGIT comes with very low expense fees.

• Energy and Mid-Stream MLP Funds

Again, under this asset class, we will have one fund that invests in midstream energy companies, and the second fund invests mainly in major oil & gas companies like Chevron (CVX) and Exxon Mobil (XOM). We are allocating a total of 10% to these two energy funds, divided equally.

1) Global X MLP & Energy Infra ETF (MLPX)

One of the major advantages of holding an MLP fund over individual midstream companies is that of tax convenience. Being an ETF, it issues a 1099-DIV instead of a cumbersome K-1 partnership income. The second advantage is that in one stroke, the fund provides diversification among the top midstream MLP (Master Limited Partnerships) companies. Another similar fund is Alerian MLP ETF (AMLP). Even though AMLP offers a higher dividend yield, it charges much higher fees and has returned lower performance on a long-term basis.

2) Adams Natural Resources Fund (PEO):

PEO is an excellent fund from the Adams group for investors who want exposure to all the major oil companies. It holds the leading oil and gas majors, such as Exxon Mobil and Chevron (CVX). In fact, the two oil majors, XOM and CVX, together represent nearly 35% of the total assets.

Another advantage that PEO offers is a high discount of roughly -9.6% plus. It also provides a very decent current yield of 9.7%. So, essentially, you are buying companies like Exxon, Chevron, and other oil majors at a large discount. Some top holdings of the fund are Exxon Mobil, Chevron (CVX), ConocoPhillips (COP), Linde (LIN), and EOG Resources (EOG).

• Real Estate funds:

We want to keep our overall portfolio diversified into many asset classes. Real Estate is a very important asset class over the long term. So, we will allocate 12% to Real Estate funds; 6% each to two funds.

Cohen & Steers Total Return Realty Fund (RFI) and C & S Real Estate Opp. & Income Fund (RLTY):

These are both from the same group with some subtle differences. RFI is quite an old fund, whereas RLTY is a fairly new fund (incepted in 2022). Also, RFI uses no leverage, while RLTY uses roughly 35% leverage. The current yields of RFI and RLTY are 8.1% and 8.6% respectively. RFI commands a small premium of 3.4% while RLTY trades at a discount of -4%.

The real estate sector and REITs have done very poorly in the last few years due to the high interest rate environment, but that is likely to change in the future when the rates start declining.

• Covered Calls Funds:

Under this category, we select two funds from the JPMorgan house. Neither of the funds has a very long history, but JEPI has nearly four years of record. As a combination of the two, it provides a wide exposure to both the S&P500 and Nasdaq-100 stocks. In addition to reasonable growth, they will likely provide a durable income.

JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPI) and JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ)

They both are income-focused options-based ETFs. JEPI is invested in large-cap S&P500 stocks, while JEPQ is mostly invested in large-cap growth stocks that are part of the Nasdaq-100 index. These funds aim to get a significant portion of the total returns of their respective indexes with lower volatility. They use a covered call strategy on the index using ELNs (Equity-Linked-Notes) to generate high income. In addition to the high income, we are also getting exposure to a large number of high-quality large-cap S&P500 and Nasdaq-100 stocks.

• Utility & Infrastructure Funds

Utilities and Infrastructure companies are known to provide stable and reliable income, albeit with low to moderate growth. However, currently, the growth expectations are also high due to rapidly increasing demand from the AI and data centers.

Reaves Utility Income (UTG) & BlackRock Utility, Infra & Power Opportunity Fund (BUI)

In our view, UTG and BUI are two of the top funds in this space. However, one key difference between the two is that UTG uses some leverage (to the extent of 20%), while BUI uses zero leverage.

Some of the top holdings of BUI are NextEra Energy (NEE), GE Vernova (GEV), National Grid Plc (NGG), Trane Technologies (TT), and Sempra (SRE).

UTG's top holdings include Talen Energy (TLN), Deutsche Telekom (OTCQX:DTEGY), Entergy (ETR), Vistra (VST), and NiSource (NI).

• Commodities and Materials:

We want our overall portfolio to have some exposure to precious metals like GOLD, and other commodities, as well as companies in the Materials sector. So, we are allocating a total of 8% to this category.

1) The Materials Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLB)

Usually, the materials sector is underrepresented in broad market indexes like the S&P 500. We are including the XLB ETF with a small allocation. XLB ETF and its underlying index seek to provide exposure to companies in the chemical, metals, mining, paper and forest products, containers and packaging, and construction material industries.

XLB charges a very low fee of 0.09%. XLB has a small number of holdings, roughly around 30. The yield of this fund is rather low at just under 2%. Its top 10 holdings represent nearly 60% of the assets, with the largest holding Linde Plc (LIN) representing 16% of the assets. The materials sector is cyclical by nature, and it can often diverge from other sectors.

2) Royal Gold Inc (RGLD) and Franco-Nevada Corp (FNV):

Note: These are the only two individual companies (not a fund) in our portfolio, but together they represent only 5% of the portfolio.

The two companies are similar and Gold royalty companies, but we are including both to provide some diversification and lower company-specific risk.

Both RGLD and FNV are mostly gold royalty and streaming companies. They both have a diversified portfolio of investments in hundreds of properties and mines. Besides providing exposure to the gold asset class, they also provide some regular income.

• Municipal and Preferreds:

1) Guggenheim Taxable Municipal & Debt Trust Fund (GBAB)

Depending upon the tax considerations, you can choose to have a taxable or non-taxable municipal fund. If you are using a tax-deferred account, a taxable fund like GBAB should be used. It currently offers a very attractive yield of roughly 9.9%. This is partly because of the suppressed valuations in the municipal and fixed-income space. As an alternative, you could use the Nuveen Taxable Municipal Income fund (NBB). Both GBAB and NBB are closed-end funds and use high leverage. GBAB appears to offer better overall returns over the long term.

2) Virtus InfraCap U.S. Preferred Stock ETF (PFFA)

PFFA is an ETF (Exchange Traded fund) and, as such, has a relatively short history (incepted May 2018). The fund primarily invests in a portfolio of "preferred" securities issued by U.S. companies that have a market capitalization of at least $100 million. The primary objective of the fund is to provide high current income. The fund is an actively managed fund and maintains a modest leverage of 20-30% to achieve its income objectives. The current yield as of Feb.03, 2025, was 9.48%. Certainly, it has higher risks due to the use of leverage.

• MISC Funds:

Under this category, we can include funds of any type. We are including a BDC-based fund for high-income and a healthcare-focused mutual fund.

1) VanEck BDC Income ETF (BIZD)

BIZD is an ETF that invests in a portfolio of BDCs and currently holds 36 individual BDCs. So, what is a BDC? A BDC (Business Development Co.) is an investment company that invests money in small and medium-sized private companies by mostly loaning money to them, but sometimes also taking equity. Generally, BDCs pay a high level of income, as they are required to pay the majority of their net income as dividends. So, there can be plenty of risks. But, BIZD being an ETF with 30 BDCs, the risk is fairly distributed.

BIZD allocates based on market-cap weighting, which results in bigger companies taking higher allocations. That's why the top three BDCs in this ETF, Ares Capital Corp (ARCC), Blue Owl Capital (OBDC), and Blackstone Secured Lending Fund (BXSL), jointly represent over 1/3rd of the assets. Currently, it also holds a large amount of treasuries, nearly 20% of the assets.

2) Janus Henderson Global Life Sciences Fund (JFNIX)

JFNIX is an open-ended equity mutual fund managed by Janus Henderson Investors LLC. This fund has an impressive record compared to other funds in its sector. It invests in stocks of companies operating across all four major segments of the healthcare sector, namely, healthcare equipment and services, pharmaceuticals, biotechnology, and life science sectors, with a slight overweight to biotechnology. It pays a variable amount of distribution, paid mostly at the year-end. Currently, the forward yield is over 6%.

Likely Risks with the Portfolio:

Here are some risks with the above portfolio that the investors should be aware of:

• Market risk: All investments are subject to risk. This portfolio is no exception, especially with 80-85% invested in equities.

• No downward protection:

The portfolio has no downward protection or hedging. During an economic downturn or some panic correction, it can have drawdowns similar to the broader market.

• Valuation risk: The overall market may be overvalued. Investors should pay attention to valuation before buying.

• Sensitivity to Interest Rates:

Many of these funds have underlying securities that are sensitive to high interest rates. If the rates were to stay high (or rise), they would be impacted negatively.

• Leverage: Leverage can make a security more volatile and can add to the risk, especially during a high-interest-rate environment. At least four of the funds in this portfolio carry leverage, though for the entire portfolio, leverage is in low single digits.

• Unexpected situations may arise as the future is always unpredictable, and the portfolio may not perform as expected.

Concluding Thoughts:

We think the portfolio of funds presented above is a highly balanced and diversified portfolio and should suit most retirees and investors who are investing for retirement. If a million dollars were invested in this portfolio, it should provide an annual income of over $72,000 (or $6,000 per month) comfortably, in addition to providing market-matching total returns over the long-term basis.

data.title: Weekly Indicators: High-Frequency And Monthly Metrics Have Diverged Sharply
data.description:
Purpose

I look at the high-frequency weekly indicators because, while they can be very noisy, they provide a good nowcast of the economy and will telegraph the maintenance or change in the economy well before monthly or quarterly data is available. They are also an excellent way to "mark your beliefs to market." In general, I go in order of long leading indicators, then short leading indicators, then coincident indicators.

A Note on Methodology

Data is presented in a "just the facts, ma'am" format with a minimum of commentary so that bias is minimized.

Where relevant, I include 12-month highs and lows in the data in parentheses to the right. All data taken from St. Louis FRED unless otherwise linked.

A few items (e.g., Financial Conditions indexes, regional Fed indexes, stock prices, the yield curve) have their own metrics based on long-term studies of their behavior.

Where data is seasonally adjusted, generally it is scored positively if it is within the top 1/3 of that range, negative in the bottom 1/3, and neutral in between. Where it is not seasonally adjusted and there are seasonal issues, waiting for the YoY change to change sign will lag the turning point. Thus, I make use of a convention: data is scored neutral if it is less than 1/2 as positive/negative as at its 12-month extreme.

With long leading indicators, which by definition turn at least 12 months before a turning point in the economy as a whole, there is an additional rule: data is automatically negative if, during an expansion, it has not made a new peak in the past year, with the sole exception that it is scored neutral if it is moving in the right direction and is close to making a new high.

For all series where a graph is available, I have provided a link to where the relevant graph can be found.

Recap of Monthly Reports

July data consisted of a very weak, barely expansionary ISM non-manufacturing index, the new orders component of which contracted for the second time in three months.

June data included a decline in both headline factory orders and core capital goods orders.

In the rearview mirror, the Q2 employment cost index rose at a 1.6% annualized rate.

Long-leading indicators

Interest rates and credit spreads

Rates

  • BAA corporate bond index 5.97%, down -0.07% w/w (1-year range: 5.33-6.43)
  • 10-year Treasury bonds 4.28%, down -0.10% w/w (3.66-4.98)
  • Credit spread 1.69%, up +0.03% w/w (1.36-2.02).

(Graph at Moody's Seasoned Baa Corporate Bond Yield | FRED | St. Louis Fed.)

Yield curve

  • 10 year minus 2 year: +0.52%, down -0.01% w/w (-1.07 - +0.53)
  • 10 year minus 3 month: +0.03%, up +0.09% w/w (-1.65 - 0.17)
  • 2 year minus Fed funds: -0.57%, up +0.08% w/w.

(Graph at 10-Year Treasury Constant Maturity Minus 2-Year Treasury Constant Maturity | FRED | St. Louis Fed.)

30-Year conventional mortgage rate (from Mortgage News Daily)

  • 6.57%, down -0.06% w/w (6.11-7.26).

Treasury and corporate bond yields, as well as mortgage rates, remain lower than their near 12-month highs of a number of months ago. The spreads between Treasuries and investment-grade and high-yield bonds continue to show little change.

The yield curve measures remain where they have been, more or less, for months, with the 10-2 year spread being the most positive and the 2 year vs. Fed funds being the most negative. The 10-year minus 3-month improved to neutral this week.

Housing

Mortgage applications (from the Mortgage Bankers Association)

  • Purchase apps up +2% to 147 (114-163) [SA]
  • Purchase apps 4 wk avg. down -4 to 149 [SA]
  • Purchase apps YoY% up +18% [NSA]
  • Purchase apps YoY 4 wk avg. +15% [NSA]
  • Refi apps up +5% w/w [SA]
  • Refi apps YoY up +15% [SA].

*[SA] = seasonally adjusted, [NSA] = not seasonally adjusted.

Real Estate Loans (from the FRB)

  • Down less than -0.1% w/w
  • Up +1.3% YoY (new multi-year low).

(Graph at Real Estate Loans, All Commercial Banks | FRED | St. Louis Fed.)

Both purchase mortgage and refinancing applications have been volatile, but within a limited range over the past 18 months. Both are now substantially higher YoY but remain close to their longer-term bottoms, so they score neutral.

In 2023, real estate loans sank below 1/2 of their 12-month high, the last housing indicator to turn negative, and have generally continued to worsen, making yet another post-pandemic low this week.

Money supply

The Federal Reserve has discontinued this weekly series. Data is now only released monthly. The June data was just released two weeks ago:

  • M1 m/m up +0.6%, YoY Real M1 up +1.6% (3-year high) (worst: -1.4% 8/23)
  • M2 m/m up +0.6%, YoY Real M2 up +1.9% (2.1% June 2025 3-year high) (worst -8.6% 9/23).

No recession has happened without a YoY real M1 negative or YoY real M2 below +2.5%. Real M2 fell below that threshold in March 2022. Real M1 also turned negative as of May 2022. In the past, when the real money supply rebounded to the levels it is at now, it was either during the latter part of a recession or in the period of recovery, so both of these are positive long-leading metrics now.

Corporate profits (56% actual + 44% estimated) from I/B/E/S via FactSet at p. 29)

  • Q2 up +1.42 to 66.39, up +4.3% q/q.

FactSet estimates earnings, which are replaced by actual earnings as they are reported, and are updated weekly. The "neutral" band is +/-3%. I also average the previous two quarters together until at least 100 companies have actually reported. In Q1, earnings wound up lower by more than -3%, which turned the rating negative. But in Q2, earnings have rebounded sharply, and as of this week, the rating has changed from neutral to positive.

Credit conditions (from the Chicago Fed)

  • Financial Conditions Index up +0.03 (less loose) to -0.54 (-0.42 -0.67)
  • Adjusted Index (removing background economic conditions) up +0.04 (less loose) to -0.48 (-0.34 - -0.61)
  • Leverage subindex up +0.03 (less loose) to -0.52 (+0.13 - -0.76).

In these indexes, lower = better for the economy. The Chicago Fed's Adjusted Index's real break-even point is roughly -0.25. In the leverage index, a negative number is good and a positive number is poor. The historical breakeven point has been -0.5 for the unadjusted Index. After briefly deteriorating to neutral several months ago, all three were recently solidly positive, but the unadjusted index remains neutral.

Short leading indicators

Economic Indicators from the late Jeff Miller’s “Weighing the Week Ahead”

  • Miller Score (formerly "C-Score"): down -12 to 404, down -1 m/m (232 8/30/24 - 421 5/16/25) Components: Yield curve 55 Phillips curve 349
  • St. Louis Fed Financial Stress Index: up +0.1175 to -0.6777 (+0.5569 4/11/25 - -.9773 1/24/25)
  • BCIp from Georg Vrba: up +3.5 to +46.0 as of 7/30/25 iM's Business Cycle Index (100 is max value, below 25 is recession signal averaging 20 weeks ahead).

The Miller Score is designed to look 52 weeks ahead for whether or not a recession is possible. Any score over 500 means no recession. This number fell below that threshold at the beginning of August 2021, so not only is it negative, but we are now years into the “recession-eligible” time period. This indicator is heavily influenced by both the yield curve and the still very low unemployment rate vs. gas prices (expecting a reversal), which explains why it remains negative. My colleague who compiles this score began disaggregating the two components several months ago.

The St. Louis Financial Stress Index is one where a negative score is a positive for the economy and during its limited existence, has risen above zero before a recession by less than one year. It remained very positive until briefly in April. It then recovered and remains among its better post-pandemic readings.

For over a year, IM has stated that its system does not forecast a recession, although in the last month, it has edged closer to its threshold.

Trade-weighted US$

  • Up +1.20 to 121.61 w/w, down -1.7% YoY (last week) (broad) (118.76 - 129.67) (Graph at Nominal Broad U.S. Dollar Index
  • Up +1.11 to 98.76 w/w, down -4.3% YoY (major currencies) (graph at link) (99.01- 109.66).

The neutral range for the US$ is between unchanged and 5% higher YoY. At 5% higher or more YoY, the strength suppresses exports and incentivizes imports, a negative for the economy. The trend in these measures had been from below 0 (thus positive) to neutral, to above 5% (negative) for 18 months, until the beginning of this year. Both measures are lower YoY now, so they are positive.

Commodity prices

Bloomberg Commodity Index

  • Up +0.17 to 100.79 (93.30 - 107.71)
  • Up +5.5% YoY.

(Graph at https://www.marketwatch.com/investing/index/bcom?countrycode=xx.)

Bloomberg Industrial metals ETF (from Bloomberg) (graph at link)

  • 141.63, up +2.19 w/w (130.47 -173.08 )
  • Up +3.6% YoY.

Beginning three months ago, the industrial measure gradually weakened from positive to negative. Then the broad commodity index declined sharply into negative territory, but then rebounded into the neutral range. By contrast, the industrial metals measures remained very negative. This can be attributed to whether or not oil is included among the commodities tracked. This week, the industrial measure turned back higher YoY and was so positive. Please note, importantly, that if commodities were not priced in US$ terms but in other major currencies, they would be lower YoY.

Stock prices: S&P 500 (from CNBC) (graph at link)

  • Up 2.4% to 6389.45.

In July and again this month, stocks have made several new all-time highs. As a result, this indicator is positive.

Regional Fed New Orders Indexes

(*indicates report this week) (no reports this week)

  • Empire State up +16.2 to 2.0
  • Philly up +16.1 to 18.4
  • Richmond down -13 to -25
  • Kansas City up +13 to +2
  • *Dallas up +3.7 to -3.6
  • Month-over-month rolling average: up +1 to -1.

The regional average is more volatile than the ISM manufacturing index but usually correctly forecasts its month-over-month direction. Since spring 2022, these have gradually declined to neutral and then negative. The indexes varied sharply last year but recently became more variable, first turning neutral and then, one month+ ago, to positive. This may have reflected manufacturers’ attempts to front-run new tariffs, which would account for the sharp reversals beginning in March.

In April, I wrote that:

“I am strongly considering adding the regional service indexes this month, to see if the consumer segment of the economy suddenly shrinks in as close to real time as reasonably available.”

Those reports are posted below, with new reports posted in the past week noted with an *:

  • Empire State up +3.9 to -9.3
  • Philly up +1.0 to -16.3
  • Richmond up +6 to -8
  • Kansas City down -5 to -2
  • *Dallas up +6.4 to +2.0
  • Average: up +1 to -7.

The regional non-manufacturing reports have stabilized at negative levels since May. Meanwhile, the ISM non-manufacturing index, averaged over three months, has continued to weaken. New orders are now negative, and the headline index is only 0.1 above its equipoise point. This means the economically weighted ISM indexes combined are showing contraction.

Employment metrics

Initial jobless claims

  • 226,000, up +7,000 w/w, down -3.4% YoY
  • 4-week average 220,750, down -500 w/w, down -7.8% YoY.

(Graph at St. Louis FRED.)

Jobless claims were positive for most of 2024, except for hurricane-influenced weeks during the autumn. They subsequently returned to neutral, then briefly positive, before turning back to neutral. We have had very good reports for the last six weeks, and two weeks ago, this indicator improved to positive.

Temporary staffing index (from the American Staffing Association) (graph at link)

  • Up +1 to 86 w/w
  • Down -1.5% YoY (low -13.6%- high -1.5% 8/7/25).

This series has been negative since early 2023. YoY comparisons continued to be negative throughout 2024. It remains frankly recessionary. I suspect this has been a secular change, giving a false signal as a result. Relatively speaking, the YoY reading has been trending “less bad” for the past number of months, and finally, since it is now more than half the way to unchanged on a YoY basis, to neutral as of earlier this year.

Tax Withholding (from the Department of the Treasury)

  • $258.7 B for the last 20 reporting days vs. $244.0 B last year, up +$14.7 B or +6.0%.

These were very positive for almost all of 2024, and with the exception of seven weeks (four after an April 15 anomaly), the trend has continued this year.

Oil prices and usage (from the E.I.A.)

  • Oil down -$3.70 to $63.61 w/w, down -9.7% YoY ($58.21 - $83.88)
  • Gas prices up +.02 to $3.14 w/w, down -$0.31 YoY
  • Usage 4-week average down -2.2% YoY.

(Information can be found at “This Week In Petroleum” at the E.I.A.’s website.)

Oil prices first declined into the bottom 1/3rd of their 3-year range and so turned positive late in 2024, and then declined even further earlier this year. After the Israeli-Iran hostilities last month, oil prices increased but have since receded back to close to four-year lows.

Gas prices also recently made new 3-year lows, and so are also positive. Last year, the mileage driven had been generally negative. It turned neutral and then positive earlier this year, but negative again last week. Since March of this year, it has oscillated between positive and negative.

Note: Given this measure’s extreme volatility, I believe the best measure is against their 3-year average.

Bank lending rates

  • 4.35% Secured Overnight Financing Rate (SOFR), down -0.04% w/w.

The TED Spread has been discontinued, and as of last September, so was LIBOR. At the suggestion of a reader, I began to track the SOFR instead. Unfortunately, SOFR has only been in existence since 2018, so there is no track record as to how it might behave around normal recessions (vs. the pandemic). Over the past 5 years, it does appear to have matched the trend in LIBOR. Given the alternatives, I am now including it in my list of indicators in the conclusion.

Coincident indicators

Tariff payments (from the Department of the Treasury)

  • June 2025 vs. June 2024: $28.0 B vs. $7.6 B (+$20.6 B)
  • July 2025 vs. July 2024: $29.6 B vs. $8.7 B (+$20.9 B)
  • August 1–6 2025 vs. August 1–6 2024: $1.5 B vs. $0.6 B (+$0.9 B).

I have inaugurated this measure temporarily since other sources, such as inferences from the CPI and PPI, are imprecise and speculative so far. The above information comes directly from the Daily Treasury Statement. These fees are either absorbed by the importer or passed on to the consumer, or some proportion of each, and are removed from potential consumption on other items. Note that the above payments do not include any from the further tariff hikes that went into effect this week.

St. Louis FRED Weekly Economic Index

  • Up +0.06 to 2.62 w/w (Low 1.65 November 2, 2024 - high 3.21 November 23, 2024).

This measure was in the neutral range during most of early 2024 but generally positive (above 2.00) since then and came very close to setting a new 12-month high three months ago before declining. After four weeks back in the neutral range, including a new 6-month low, seven weeks ago, it returned to positive territory.

Restaurant reservations YoY (from Open Table) State of the Restaurant Industry | OpenTable

  • Last week - 7-day average up +15% YoY, vs. +13% one week prior.

Since 2023, this index has been very volatile, swinging between positive, neutral, and negative several times. In general, earlier this year, the YoY trends improved, but with a great deal of noise. In the past two months, however, this metric has been trending sharply higher.

Consumer spending

  • Johnson Redbook up +6.5% YoY, 4-week average +5.4% (high 7.4% 4/25/25; 4.8% 2/1 and 7/3, 2025) United States Redbook Index.

The Redbook index has been positive since February last year. It had very good readings in April and early May, probably due to consumers front-running anticipated tariff price increases and shortages. Since then, it had been weakening—until three weeks ago, when it stabilized. This week it increased sharply.

Consumer inflation by Truflation (Independent, economic & financial data in real time on-chain | Truflation)

  • Up +0.10% to +1.77% YoY (High 3.11% 12/11/24 - Low 1.06% 9/13/24).

This recent addition is a daily update to inflation, similar to the “billion price project” of the last decade (which required a subscription). I have not added this to my list below of coincident or leading indicators, but needless to say, it is an up-to-the-moment reading on this very important indicator. This series has been volatile, and the YoY comparisons have been turning higher since September but have been very low in the last several months, although it did make another new two-month high ten weeks ago.

Real Consumer Spending

  • Up +4.7% YoY (high - 6.0% 4/25/25; 12-month low 1.7%% 12/16/24).

This metric premiered at the beginning of last year. One of my most important mantras is that consumption leads to employment. Real retail sales have a long history of doing so, but are only reported on a monthly basis.

The weekly result is derived simply by subtracting YoY inflation as measured by Truflation from the YoY change in nominal consumer spending as measured by Redbook. While it will be somewhat noisy, it should anticipate changes in the monthly measures ahead of time. It backed off from the 12-month high it set in February and then gradually rebounded and made another 12-month high several months ago. After mainly weeks in the upper part of its 12-month range, it was tepid at the beginning of the year before improving in the past two months, even making a new 12-month high before backing off. With nominal spending relatively low and inflation holding generally steady, this indicator weakened, although still positive. It has improved considerably along with nominal consumer spending in the past month.

Transport

Railroads (from the AAR)

  • Carloads up +6.4% YoY (cumulative 2025 vs. 2024 up +2.8%)
  • Intermodal units up +0.2% YoY (cumulative up +4.7%)
  • Total loads up +2.9% YoY (cumulative up +3.8%).

(Graph at Railfax Report - North American Rail Freight Traffic Carloading Report.)

Shipping transport

  • Harpex unchanged at 2184 (new 24-month high)
  • Baltic Dry Index up +5 to 2008.

Rail was mixed for many weeks before turning positive in March, presumably reflecting businesses attempting to get inventory before the new tariffs kick in. Intermodal rail loads had steadily decelerated since then, and five weeks ago turned negative. The total number of total loads was negative for several weeks as well. All three measures improved sharply in July and remained positive this week.

Harpex was as high as nearly 4500 almost 2 years ago but declined as low as 810 in December of last year before rising, setting repeated 12-month highs in the past four months. The usual interpretation for this is that demand is straining against supply, showing a strong global economy and thus very positive.

Similarly, the BDI was in a generally declining trend throughout 2022 before bottoming at a three-year low of 530 in February 2023. The overall trend has been higher since then, rising to a high of 3346 last December, probably reflecting the Houthis attacks on shipping in the Red Sea. This year, its low was 1309 in January, and its recent high was 2419 last March. It declined to the bottom 1/3rd of its one-year range during the rest of the year but rebounded all the way to the top of its six-month range earlier this year before retreating into the middle of that range.

I am wary of reading too much into price indexes like this, since they are heavily influenced by supply (as in, a huge overbuilding of ships in the last decade) as well as demand.

Steel production (American Iron and Steel Institute)

  • Up +0.3% w/w
  • Up +4.5% YoY.

This metric has been very volatile for over a year. Recently, it improved to neutral and then positive for two weeks before backing off to neutral again in May. Last week, its YoY measure was sufficiently small to change the rating to neutral, and three weeks ago it returned to positive.

Summary and Conclusion

Below are this week’s spreadsheets of the long leading, short leading, and coincident readings. Check marks indicate the present reading. If there has been a change this week, the prior reading is marked with an X:

.

The long leading indicators continue to be very mixed. Meanwhile, the short-leading indicators turned more positive this week, buoyed by a rise in industrial commodity prices and a decline in oil prices. Coincident indicators remain extremely positive, particularly helped by strong consumer spending.

This method of looking at high-frequency weekly data is only one of three systems I use to forecast the economy. Last week I wrote that:

“The monthly data, as opposed to this weekly data, is at very least flashing yellow, and within the next week could be flashing red, i.e., recession watch.”

That indeed happened this week, as the ISM services index was just barely outside of contraction territory, and its new orders subindex was contractionary again. Thus, the combined ISM indexes, weighted by their sectors’ respective contributions to the economy, are on net in contraction for the first time since the pandemic. As noted last week, the leading indicators from the employment report were also negative. Several other important monthly datapoints, like heavy truck sales, as well as housing metrics, are also in recession territory.

In short, there is a very significant divergence between the above weekly data and most monthly data. Indeed, the strength in commodity prices may be mainly due to the weakness in the US$. Priced in other currencies, commodities would be negative YoY. In other words, the “true” signal might be very unusual — i.e., global weakness caused in part by a faltering US$. But so long as the stock market keeps making new record highs and initial jobless claims stay well behaved, it is difficult to see any imminent recession.

data.title: Itafos: Cheap And Overlooked With Aligned Managers
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Kenny Chan as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Business

Itafos Inc. (OTCPK:ITFS, TSXV:IFOS:CA) is a Houston-headquartered phosphate fertilizer producer and one of four key producers in the US, representing 7% of the phosphate market’s capacity. It produces in Idaho with its vertically integrated business Conda, where the company mines and processes phosphate rock to produce monoammonium phosphate (MAP), superphosphoric acid (SPA), and byproducts.

MAP is the second most widely used form of phosphate fertilizer, used with almost all types of crops. SPA is a key ingredient used to manufacture liquid phosphate fertilizers like ammonium polyphosphate.

Phosphorus is an essential macronutrient for crops, and there are no substitutes for phosphate fertilizers. Itafos has a five-year contract with J.R. Simplot, that began in 2024, to sell all of its MAP production, while SPA is sold on the spot market.

The company’s current mine in Rasmussen Valley is expected to produce until mid-2026. A separate mine called H1/NDR has been developed and is expected to start producing in mid-2025 and produce through mid-2037. Development of Conda will continue into 2027 with $6-8 million of annual capex to start on extending the project’s life to the middle of the century and beyond. Conda made up 95% of revenues in fiscal 2024.

Management claims that their facility is notably more efficient than The Mosaic Company (MOS) and Nutrien Ltd. (NTR), the two publicly traded players, due to proper maintenance spending. Figures provided by Itafos show that it boasts higher utilization rates and EBITDA margins. Itafos has said that this is because maintenance capex is their priority in capital allocation. On the other hand, the largest player in the industry, Mosaic, recently announced $100 million in investments to restore capacity in their phosphate facilities after maintenance issues.

Outside the US, Itafos owns two phosphate projects in Brazil and one project in Guinea-Bissau. Their Brazilian Arraias facility currently produces sulfuric acid and is being restarted to produce fertilizer, though it will likely just be a small bump to earnings due to low phosphate quality. The other Brazilian asset Santana and the Guinea-Bissau asset Farim are in the development stage and would require a large amount of capital to start up. Management has publicly said that they are exploring options for monetizing these assets.

The company also holds around 10% of outstanding shares, additional options, and rights in St. George Mining after selling their rare earths project Araxa to them earlier this year. This stake is currently worth around $5-$6 million. St. George is expected to release a preliminary feasibility study on the project in late 2025.

Market

Supply and demand trends for phosphate rock and fertilizer are incredibly strong in the US and globally.

In the US, the majority of phosphate rock consumed comes from domestic production, with an expected 23 Mt consumed and only 3.5 Mt imported in 2024, according to the US Geological Survey. More than 95% of raw phosphate rock mined in the US was used in feedstock, primarily for phosphate fertilizers. The majority of finished phosphate fertilizer products are produced domestically as well, with 6 million short tons produced in the US compared with 1.4 million imported in 2023, according to the USDA.

The US market is somewhat insulated from the global market due to countervailing duties and tariffs. In 2021, the US imposed these countervailing duties on phosphate fertilizer imports from Morocco and Russia, which currently sit at rates ranging from 16.6% to 47%. The DOC and ITC are currently undergoing the five-year sunset review on these countervailing duties to determine if they will remain. In 2019, before the countervailing duties, Morocco and Russia made up 80% of phosphate fertilizer imports, according to the USDA. In 2024, they only made up 4.5%. The baseline Liberation Day tariffs will likely reduce imports from the remaining countries.

Therefore, US production trends are incredibly important to the market. Production of MAP and DAP, the two most commonly used types of phosphate fertilizer, has fallen by almost half since 2010, according to The Fertilizer Institute. This is most likely due to declining phosphate rock supply, particularly high-quality phosphate rock, and capital restraints on building new projects. US Geological Survey (USGS) data shows a decline in production of phosphate ore from around 50 Mt in the early 1980s to around 20 Mt in 2023. A 1984 USGS Circular quoted predictions at the time, since proven to be true, that “easily minable, low-cost reserves of phosphate rock would be exhausted, and that by the end of [the] century, instead of being a major exporter of phosphate rock, the US might become a net importer.”

Even for the remaining international exposure, global phosphate trends are also very strong. Mosaic expects global supply will not be able to meet demand even if demand grows slower than historically. Phosphate demand is being driven by population growth and biofuel growth. Notably, Mosaic claims that grain and oilseed yields need to accelerate from a 0.4% growth rate since 2000 to a 1.2% growth rate to meet the food and fuel demand, which necessitates fertilizers. Additionally, more lithium-iron phosphate production for electric vehicles and energy storage will take away from global phosphate supply.

Mosaic also expects that China will decrease its phosphate fertilizer exports by 25% in the next 5 years. The country made up 24% of global exports in the past 3 years, according to The Fertilizer Institute, and has been limiting phosphate exports through quotas since 2022. It is doing so to protect domestic food security and phosphate prices.

On the demand side, the US farming industry has held much steadier than the phosphate industry for the past two decades. Principal crops by acres planted have fallen marginally through the years, especially during floods in 2019. Planted acres fell from 328 million acres in 2000 to 315 million in 2024, according to the USDA.

These trends are unlikely to change in the next few years due to the development time of phosphate mines. Even after discovery and a feasibility study, it can take 8 years to prepare a mine for production in the US due to permitting, engineering, and financing. Even a state-run corporation like Morocco’s OCP Group can take 3-5 years to get a mine ready. A new mine will then need 1-2 years to ramp up production to full capacity.

Reason for Undervaluation

Until recently, Itafos was essentially an investment vehicle for their current majority owner, Castlelake, an asset management firm that still owns around 65% of outstanding shares. Itafos hasn’t made any earnings calls and hasn’t gone to any investor conferences until a recent Sidoti conference visit. This lack of communication was combined with unprofitability until 2021 and a meaningful debt balance in the past.

Today, Castlelake still holds two board seats. Management says that Itafos was originally part of their now wound-down natural resource arm. It is not a fund-constraint position for them. Itafos said during the Sidoti presentation that Castlelake is an “active participant in trying to find ways to unlock value”.

Valuation

It is extremely difficult to find a proper price target for Itafos since there are few comparable companies and the two public players trade at a premium multiple unrealistic for Itafos’s size. However, a 4x P/E ratio based on LTM earnings is far too cheap, especially because MAP prices are meaningfully higher than they were in 2024. The company guides a similar production level in 2025 as was produced in 2024.

At a reasonable 10x P/E on LTM earnings, Itafos would trade at around a USD $1 billion market cap.

Risks

Itafos and phosphate prices are highly dependent on the US farming industry. In 2019, major flooding led to more than 19.4 million acres to be unplanted, according to the USDA, the most since the figure started to be reported in 2007. Weakened US demand coincided with a sharp increase in phosphate fertilizer exports globally. This led to the DAP spot price in the US Gulf to fall from a peak of around $420/metric ton in September 2018 to around $240 in December 2019. DAP price is highly correlated with other phosphate fertilizers like MAP. Though countervailing duties and tariffs are currently in place to insulate the US from global price pressure, another major weather disaster could be impactful to Itafos.

The current threat of tariffs on US agricultural products could also damage the US farming industry. A USDA article from April 2025 reports that “since 2013, the share of U.S. agricultural and food production (in terms of value) sold in international markets has remained steady at approximately 20 percent.”

The impact of the trade war during Trump’s first presidency was significant for certain U.S. crops, particularly soybeans, which were directly targeted by Chinese tariffs. However, there was minimal impact on DAP prices in 2018, when tariffs were first implemented on US agriculture. Eventually, the US came to a trade agreement with trade partners during the first trade war and reimbursed farmers through the Market Facilitation Programs. Of course, things could be different today if the countries don’t reach an agreement with the US.

There have been conversations about a program similar to the Market Facilitation Program, implemented after tariffs on the US in 2018, to reimburse farmers for tariff losses, though nothing concrete yet. Regardless of a tariff relief program, the US has already planned extensive subsidies to farms in 2025. The USDA forecasts direct government payments of $42.4 billion for 2025, a $33.1 billion increase from 2024.

Separately, a decision to sunset countervailing duties on Morocco and Russia would be impactful as well, but seems unlikely given the administration’s stance on domestic production.

It is also important to note the cash expenditures expected in the next few years. Itafos expects capex to rise to over $100 million in 2025. This includes $6-8 million in annual capex that will be deployed from 2025-2027 to start expansion of mine life past 2037. Also, Itafos will likely start paying cash payments on environmental and asset retirement obligations after the close of the Rasmussen Valley mine.

Other risks include expanding global supply, though there would be visibility into this due to the delay before supply can emerge.

Conclusion

At a 4x P/E, Itafos is too cheap to ignore. Phosphate demand and supply trends suggest an increasing long-term price, which will translate to growth for Itafos. Additionally, management seems to be engaged in unlocking value.

data.title: BDC Weekly Review: Tight Spreads Keep A Lid On Net Income
data.description:
Welcome to another installment of our BDC Market Weekly Review, where we discuss market activity in the Business Development Company [BDC] sector from both the bottom-up, highlighting individual news and events-as well as the top-down-providing an overview of the broader market.

We also try to add some historical context as well as relevant themes that look to be driving the market or that investors ought to be mindful of. This update covers the period through the first week of August.

Market Action

BDCs had a tough week, underperforming the broader income market. This was despite strong early earnings numbers from the sector. All names in our coverage were lower, with PSEC falling by double-digit figures. Year-to-date, the average stock in our coverage is flat, though dispersion is very high.

The average valuation in our coverage fell below 100% and is a few percentage points below the longer-term average figure.

Market Themes

One interesting piece of data in the Sixth Street Specialty Lending (TSLX) Q2 earnings release was the weighted-average spread of new commitments. This was 6.7% in Q2, below the 7% level in Q1 which was the highest over the previous year.

This, admittedly anecdotal piece of evidence, suggests that the market volatility in April was much too brief to allow BDCs to lend at favorable spreads. With short-term rates having been flat for some time, secondary factors like lending spreads, leverage, and interest expense have come to the fore in driving BDC net income figures. The fact that spreads are relatively tight, if not at the lowest historical levels, suggests that net income will continue to face headwinds.

Other evidence suggests that current BDC sector spreads are below portfolio weighted-average levels. This means that as loans are repaid and BDCs make new loans, the overall level of net income falls slightly. This will not have a huge impact on BDC net income, however, until the Fed makes its next move, it will continue to weigh on income levels in the sector.

Market Commentary

Hercules Capital (HTGC) delivered stellar results with a 6.9% quarterly return. The NAV jumped 2.5% - one of the strongest rises in the last few years.

There was a chunky net realized loss; however, it was more than made up by unrealized appreciation. Unrealized appreciation remains a key theme of this young earnings season, boosting overall returns in the sector. Leverage remained modest, below 1x with low non-accruals at just 0.2%.

Ares Capital Corp.'s (ARCC) core EPS came in unchanged from Q1. The NAV rose 0.4% for a total NAV return of 2.9% for the quarter. This was above the +2.1% Q1 figure but below the prior two quarters. Overall, a very solid double-digit annualized number.

Dividend coverage was 104%, and this will move below 100% if the Fed continues to deliver the 3-4 rate cuts that the market expects. The most recent payrolls number suggests we will see another cut from the Fed in September.

The company has quite a bit of spillover left, so it will try to avoid cutting the dividend if it can. Non-accruals rose slightly to 1.2% on fair-value from 0.9%. Overall, a very good result. The stock is fairly expensive at a 13% premium - 20% above the median figure - the highest in a long time.

Stance And Takeaways

Though the Q2 earnings season has only begun and we have only seen the stronger BDCs report, so far there is good reason for cautious optimism. As we suggested earlier, the tightening in public credit spreads during the quarter should support NAVs via unrealized appreciation, and this is what we are seeing. This means that despite the headwinds of tight spreads, falling short-term rates, and a low level of M&A activity, BDCs continue to deliver very attractive returns for investors while limiting their exposure to higher longer-term rates.
